coredata.dc:title: Study of heat and mass transfer processes during extraction of plant raw materials under the influence of ultrasound
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Korobiichuk, Igor
Mel'nick, Viktorij
Ostapenko, Zhanna
Ruzhinska, Ludmila
coredata.dc:description: Theoretical analysis of the process of biologically active substances (BAS) extraction from plant raw materials in conditions of ultrasonic action and without it to describe the kinetics of the process has been conducted. A mathematical model of the process of BAS extraction from plant raw materials to establish the dependence of changes in the concentration of BAS in the volume of cells in the intercellular space and in the main volume of the extractant has been developed. On the basis of the solution of the mathematical model the duration of the model of BAS extraction process from plant raw materials has been established, results show that the duration of the process of extraction of oil from plant raw materials in an acoustic extractor decreases by 1.5 times ultrasonic extraction can be used for the extraction of biologically active substances, such as essential oils, lipids and dietary supplements from plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasonic extraction
Biologically active substances
Mathematical model
Acoustic extractor
Raw materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of diffusion coefficients of the extraction process from plant raw materials under ultrasonic conditions
Determination of mass transfer coefficients and extraction time

Values of coefficients

.

head.ce:title: Study of heat and mass transfer processes during extraction of plant raw materials under the influence of ultrasound

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Łukasiewicz Research Network – Industrial Research Institute for Automation and Measurements PIAP, Warsaw, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Łukasiewicz Research Network – Industrial Research Institute for Automation and Measurements PIAP

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Łukasiewicz Research Network – Industrial Research Institute for Automation and Measurements PIAP, Warsaw, Poland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Technical University of Ukraine “Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Polytechnic Institute”, Kyiv, Ukraine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Technical University of Ukraine “Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Polytechnic Institute”

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Technical University of Ukraine “Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Polytechnic Institute”, Kyiv, Ukraine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Theoretical analysis of the process of biologically active substances (BAS) extraction from plant raw materials in conditions of ultrasonic action and without it to describe the kinetics of the process has been conducted. A mathematical model of the process of BAS extraction from plant raw materials to establish the dependence of changes in the concentration of BAS in the volume of cells in the intercellular space and in the main volume of the extractant has been developed. On the basis of the solution of the mathematical model the duration of the model of BAS extraction process from plant raw materials has been established, results show that the duration of the process of extraction of oil from plant raw materials in an acoustic extractor decreases by 1.5 times ultrasonic extraction can be used for the extraction of biologically active substances, such as essential oils, lipids and dietary supplements from plants.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most promising physical methods of intensification of extraction processes is ultrasound. Propagation of sound waves in the medium of the apparatus occurs by periodic liquefaction and compression of the medium with a frequency corresponding to the oscillation frequency of the sound wave and the amplitude with a rarefaction equal to the compression amplitude. During operation of the ultrasonic generator there are effects of mixing and heating of the medium, as well as the emergence of pulsation, collapse of the ensemble of cavitation bubbles.

Extraction under the action of ultrasound has its own advantages. The features of ultrasonic extraction are that the equipment has the possibility of continuous operation and is suitable for mass extraction of drugs from plants; the extraction time is reduced; the process takes place at a low temperature, which is very important for the extraction of thermolabile pharmaceutical ingredients. Sound waves with frequencies above 20 kHz are mechanical vibrations in solids, liquids and gases. Unlike electromagnetic waves, sound waves travel in any matter, they are associated with cycles of compression and expansion as they move through the medium. Ultrasound can be used to lower the operating temperature of thermolabile extraction. In addition, any solvent can be used in ultrasonic extraction.
Ultrasonic extraction can be used for the extraction of BAS, such as essential oils, lipids and dietary supplements from plants.

Given the increasing man-made load on the environment and the threat of depletion of non-renewable natural resources, today there is a need to create and introduce into production resource-saving energy-efficient and environmentally friendly technologies.

One of the problems that innovative methods and technologies are aimed at solving is the maximum extraction of useful products from raw materials. This refers to the extraction of useful substances from plant raw materials.

Extraction of active components from plant products using an appropriate solvent is a classic operation in pharmaceutical production. Solid-liquid solvent extraction is an operation found in many industrial processes. The pharmaceutical industry, as well as the perfume or pesticide industries, are faced with extracting active ingredients from plant materials, which usually contain small amounts of active solute. [1].

Conventional extraction methods used for solvent extraction of plant raw materials have been proven to have longer extraction times, higher temperatures and lower extraction efficiency, in particular, negative effects on natural components due to the use of large amounts of organic solvents and higher energy consumption [2,3]. In addition, some natural components are thermolabile and can be destroyed during thermal extraction [4,5].

Various methods of extracting biologically active substances (BAS) from plant raw materials have already been reported in the literature, including conventional solvent extraction, Soxhlet extraction, thermal reflux extraction, continuous microextraction, and enzyme-assisted extraction [6–10]. In addition, advanced extraction methods based on microwave extraction [11], supercritical fluid carbon dioxide extraction [7], high voltage electric discharge extraction [12], and ultrasonic extraction [13–15] are presented.

Extraction by ultrasound is evaluated as a simpler and more effective alternative to conventional extraction methods for the extraction of BAS from a natural product [16–21]. This method is a “green” process that consumes less energy and time and provides a higher yield of the extract.
Ultrasound are used in various chemical engineering operations: wastewater treatment, drying, sonochemistry, and solid–liquid extraction [22–24].

In the field of agriculture the effect of ultrasound on a large variety of rigid substrates has been studied, which in some cases shows a positive effect. Indeed, ultrasound treatment of grain increases its kinetic growth and thus its yield. It also seems that enzymes, which are the key to biochemical reactions, increase their activity when ultrasound is well distributed.

It has been proven that ultrasound is a very useful means of increasing the efficiency of BAS extraction from natural products and improving mass transfer in the liquid medium [25–30]. The increased extraction rate is associated with a phenomenon called acoustic cavitation caused by the propagation of ultrasonic pressure waves, which can cause drastic destruction of the plant cell wall and cell membrane as well as a decrease in particle size [27,29]. In addition, some positive effects, such as fluid circulation fluxes and turbulence generated by cavitation, facilitate the penetration of solvents into cellular materials, which leads to a significant improvement in mass transfer rates, which contributes to higher extraction yields [30–32]. This effect is much stronger at low frequencies (18–40 kHz) and virtually insignificant at 400–800 kHz. [16].

The physical effects occurring during ultrasonic extraction cause the cavitation process, which are dominant when operating at low frequencies. Because of this problem, low-frequency ultrasound is recommended for the extraction of phytochemicals from plants. On the other hand, the efficiency of extraction with ultrasound depends on the ability of the solvent to absorb and transfer the energy of ultrasound. [33–37].

Most of the work associated with ultrasonic extraction has been done with a low-frequency ultrasonic emitter and an ultrasonic bath. Ultrasonic baths are more widely used because direct ultrasonic treatment can affect the quality of the extracted ingredients and can be harmful to thermolabile materials because they cause intense cavitation. [38].

The results obtained by ultrasonic extraction had a lower extraction temperature, a much shorter time, and a higher flavonoid yield than conventional methods. The maximum amount of flavonoid extract (9.263 mg/g) (solvent −70% ethanol, particle size −0.285 mm, solvent/solids ratio −40 ml/g, extraction temperature −55 °C, ultrasound power −120 W and ultrasound frequency −45 kHz) was obtained in only 80 min, showing that ultrasound can be used to improve extraction efficiency and reduce the extraction time of flavonoids.

The effect of ultrasound on saponin extraction from ginseng was studied [39]. Saponin extraction was studied as a function of irradiation time and acoustic pressure. It was observed that the yield of both total extract and saponin was greater with ultrasonic irradiation than without ultrasonic irradiation. The increase in the yield of the total extract is about 15%, and for saponin it is about 30%.

In [40] it is reported that ultrasound treatment of grapes for 5–10 min (frequency −80 kHz) improves the taste of wine and its lighting. Application of ultrasound during short maceration periods was effective in obtaining greater alkaloid yield from Datura stramonium.

In addition to the beneficial effects of ultrasound on extraction yield and kinetics, it allows the experimental conditions to be altered, such as lowering temperature and pressure. Then the quality of the dissolved substance, as in the case of protein extraction from autoclaved soy flakes, can be preserved [41].

Ultrasound is elastic oscillations and waves with a frequency greater than 15–20 kHz. The upper limit of ultrasonic frequencies is due to the physical nature of elastic waves, which can only propagate in a material medium, i.e. under the condition that the wavelength is much longer than the free path of molecules in the gas or the interatomic distances in liquids and solids. In gases at normal pressure the upper frequency limit of ultrasonic frequencies is 109 Hz, in liquids and solids the frequency limit reaches 1012-1013 Hz. Depending on the wavelength and frequency, ultrasound has different specific characteristics of emission, reception, propagation and application, so the region of ultrasonic frequencies are divided into three regions:
•
low frequencies (ultrasonic 15–102 Hz);
•
middle frequencies (102–104 Hz);
•
high frequencies (104–106 Hz).
Elastic waves with frequencies of 109–1013 Hz are commonly referred to as hypersonic.

As for physical properties, ultrasonic waves are not inherently different from the elastic waves of the audible range. Only longitudinal waves propagate in gases and liquids, while in solids only longitudinal and shear waves propagate [42].

Ultrasound propagation obeys the basic laws common to acoustic waves of any frequency range. The basic laws of propagation include the laws of sound reflection and refraction of sound at the boundaries of different media, sound diffraction and sound scattering in the presence of interference and inhomogeneity in the medium and inequalities on the boundaries, the laws of wave propagation in limited areas of the medium. An essential role is played by the relationship between the wavelength of sound and geometric D-size - the size of the sound source or interference in the path of the wave, the size of inhomogeneity of the medium. The speed of propagation of ultrasonic waves in an unbounded medium is determined by the elasticity and density of the medium.

In confined media, the velocity of wave propagation is influenced by the presence and nature of boundaries, which leads to a frequency dependence of the velocity (sound velocity dispersion). Decrease in the amplitude and intensity of ultrasonic waves as it propagates in a given direction, i.e. sound attenuation, is caused, as for waves of any frequency, divergence of the wave front with distance from the source, scattering and absorption of sound. At all frequencies of both audible and inaudible ranges, there is so-called “classical” absorption caused by viscosity (internal friction) of the medium. In addition, there is additional (relaxation) absorption, often significantly exceeding the “classical” absorption [43].

The process of BAS extraction from plant raw materials with organic solvents such as extraction gasoline, methylene chloride, dichloroethane is carried out by mass transfer, in particular by convective diffusion on the surface of solid raw material particles and molecular diffusion inside the particles. The diffusion process depends on the size of the raw material particles, temperature, properties of the solvent, such as viscosity and the diffusion coefficient of BAS inside and outside the solid particles.

The extraction process, according to current ideas, occurs by penetration of solvent between the particles and dissolved in the solvent BAS that are on the outer surface of the particles, passing the solvent inside the solid particles and dissolution of BAS that are inside.

In the extractant flow, the BAS moves from the inner layers of the particle to the surface in the pores and capillaries and through the undamaged cell walls. Then the BAS enters the surface of the particles in the boundary layer and in the main flow of the extractant moving in the extraction apparatus.

For a qualitative assessment of mass transfer in the process of extraction from plant raw materials, the authors of [44] proposed an equation for determining the mass transfer coefficient:
(1)
K =
1
1
n
D b
+
δ D
+
1 β
,
where Db – diffusion coefficient of the BAS solution inside the particles of solid raw material;
D – diffusion coefficient of BAS solution through the boundary layer;
β – convective diffusion coefficient;
δ – thickness of boundary layer at the surface of solid raw material.
The coefficien Db depends on the temperature, the viscosity of the solvent, and the size of the molecules of the diffusing substance, i.e. the molecules of the BAS solution.

The component
δ D
characterizes the effect on the process of mass transfer of physical properties of the solvent, BAS solution, temperature and speed of motion of the solution (place).
The coefficient β reflects the effect on the mass transfer of hydrodynamic conditions of the process, velocity, mode of movement of the sites, physical properties and temperature.

It was found that the presence of undestroyed cells, the presence of secondary partitions inside the rigid particles worsens the extraction process. Destructive phenomena caused by the action of ultrasonic cavitation contribute to intensification of internal mass transfer. In addition, cavitation leads to the formation of additional microcracks on the solid material surface and increases the pressure, which in turn intensifies the process of solvent penetration into the pores of the solid particles.

Increasing the temperature of the solvent during the ultrasonic action reduces the viscosity of the extractant and improves its movement both within the pores of the solids and in the boundary layer and increases the diffusion rate.

Analysis of factors affecting the intensity of the extraction process shows that the processing of raw materials of plant origin has certain features associated with the capillary-porous structure formed by cells of different structures, which contain the target product. Particles of plant raw materials, from which the target product is extracted, conventionally refers to solids. Depending on the state of the raw material (dry, moisture) extraction is divided into different number of consecutive stages. For the extraction of dried raw materials there are the following stages [45]:
–
penetration of the solvent into the pores of the raw material particles;
–
dissolution of the target component;
–
transfer of the extracted substance from the surface of the raw material particles into the extractant mass.
Dissolution of the target product on the walls of the destroyed cells depends on the hydrodynamic conditions of washing the raw material particles with the extractant.
Transfer of the target product through the wall of the plant cells is considered as a process of mass transfer through a semipermeable membrane.

Mass transfer from the surface of solids to the solution of the target product in the extractant depends on the hydrodynamic conditions of washing the particles with the solution and occurs mainly due to convective diffusion. The influence of molecular diffusion is insignificant at creation of intensive movement of solution through the layer of raw material particles. To intensify the process of mass transfer from the surface of solids into solution, characterized by mass transfer coefficient, it is necessary to ensure the penetration of solution through the particles, prevent tracking of solid particles in the layer, blocking of particle surface by other particles, changing the position of particles in the layer, which is achieved not only by movement of solution through the layer, but also by action of ultrasonic generator.

To design new high-performance equipment it is necessary to conduct a theoretical analysis of technological features of the process of BAS extraction from plant raw materials in an acoustic extractor by mathematical modeling of the process.

Duration of the extraction process is determined by the mass transfer rate inside the particle of plant raw materials, the resistance of the diffusion layer of the particle surface and the convective resistance in the extractant.
Let us introduce the following notation:

V
ek
– volume of liquid circulating per unit time through the acoustic extractor. During the time
Δ τ
there is a volume
V
ek

ϑ
ek
· Δ τ
in the extractor;
K – mass transfer coefficient from the particle of solid phase (raw material) to the extractant;
Cmb – current concentration of BAS in the raw material;

V
mb
– volume of solid particles of raw materials.

Then the mass of BAS entering per unit time in the extractant is determined by the expression:
(2)
Δ
m

V

where
C

C

– concentration of BAS in the raw material solid particles and the extractant, respectively.

In terms of mass transfer phenomena, the mechanism of BAS extraction from a single particle of plant raw materials consists of the process of penetration of the extractant into the spaces between the particles and into the pores of raw materials due to capillary forces. The extractant dissolves the BAS on the surface of the particles, on the pore walls and in the depth of the particle. [44,46,47].

BAS dissolved in the extractant move to the surface of the particle in pores, capillaries through unbroken cell walls and partitions of secondary structures. Through the boundary or diffuse liquid layer surrounding the BAS particle in the solvent flow, it exits to the particle surface and then enters the solvent flow moving through the solid particle layer.

For a qualitative and quantitative description of the process of mass transfer during extraction, they use [44,46,47]:
Internal diffusion coefficient
D
bn
, which characterizes the rate of mass transfer within the particle of plant raw materials.
Free diffusion coefficient
D
md
, which characterizes the mass transfer in the cell sap and the diffusion boundary layer.
Convective diffusion coefficient β.

The mass transfer coefficient of extraction from plant raw materials is determined by the formula [44,46,47]:
(9)
K =
1
1 β
+
l
D
bn
+
s
D
md
where
D
md
– molecular diffusion coefficient;
β – coefficient of convective diffusion;
l – particle size of raw material;
s – thickness of the boundary layer.

Molecular diffusion coefficient [46,47]:
(10)
D
md
=
k 0
· T
6 π μ r
where
k 0
– Boltzmann constant;
T – absolute temperature of the process;
r – radius of diffusing particles;
μ – viscosity of liquid medium.

The coefficient of internal diffusion in pores of plant material differs from the coefficient of free diffusion and is defined by the equation [44,46,47]:
(11)
D
bn
=
k 0
· T · B
6 π μ r
where B – correction factor.
In the absence of B value, the coefficient Dbn is determined experimentally.

For a layer of spherical solid particles the volume and number of particles and their specific surface are determined by the formulas [48,49]:

To determine the duration of the BAS extraction process, we solve the equation describing the change in the concentration of BAS in solid slices during extraction. [48]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of diffusion coefficients of the extraction process from plant raw materials under ultrasonic conditions
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of mass transfer coefficients and extraction time
ce:section.ce:para:
The calculations were performed in the MathCad 15 software environment. The calculations were performed for the values of
W
=
0 , 015
m
s
,
W
=
0 , 02
m
s
,
W
=
0 , 03
m
s
Table 3 .
Diffusion coefficients, mass transfer coefficients, and extraction time were calculated using the obtained dependencies.

At conventional extraction the extraction time was
τ
K
= 56 m i n
, and at ultrasonic extraction –
τ k
= 37 m i n
. Consequently, when using ultrasound, the extraction time of BAS from plant raw materials decreased by 1.5 times. Therefore, we can increase the productivity of the extractor by 1.5 times compared to apparatuses without ultrasound.

ce:list-item.ce:para: A mathematical model of the process of BAS extraction from plant raw materials to establish the dependence of changes in the concentration of BAS in the volume of cells in the intercellular space and in the main volume of the extractant has been developed.

Based on the solution of the mathematical model and experimental data, the diffusion coefficients of BAS solution through the cell membrane and diffusion coefficients in the intercellular space have been determined.
On the basis of the solution of the mathematical model the duration of the model of BAS extraction process from plant raw materials has been established.
Calculations using the experimentally found values of coefficients of BAS solution diffusion through the cell membrane and diffusion coefficients in the intercellular space have been made.

Calculation results show that the duration of the process of extraction of oil from plant raw materials in an acoustic extractor decreases by 1.5 times, which occurs in an extractor without the use of ultrasound.

ce:section.ce:para: This research received no external funding. The APC was funded by Łukasiewicz Research Network—Industrial Research Institute for Automation and Measurements PIAP.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation in solid–liquid extraction: influence of ultrasound

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Romdhane, C. Gourdon, Investigation in solid–liquid extraction: influence of ultrasound, Chemical Engineering Journal, vol. 87, (2002), pp. 11–19

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasound-synergized electrostatic field extraction of total flavonoids from Hemerocallis citrina baroni

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.F. Yang, L.L. Geng, H.Q. Lu, X.D. Fan, Ultrasound-synergized electrostatic field extraction of total flavonoids from Hemerocallis citrina baroni, Ultrason. Sonochem., vol. 34, (2017), pp. 571–579.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Process intensification and kinetic studies of ultrasound-assisted extraction of flavonoids from peanut shells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jianqing Liao , Zhouren Guo , Guicai Yu, Process intensification and kinetic studies of ultrasound-assisted extraction of flavonoids from peanut shells, Ultrasonics Sonochemistry, vol. 76, (2021), 105661.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Mohammad Chand Ali, Jia Chen, Haijuan Zhang, Zhan Li, Liang Zhao, Hongdeng Qiu, Effective extraction of flavonoids from Lycium barbarum L. fruits by deep eutectic solvents-based ultrasound-assisted extraction, Talanta, vol. 203, (2019), pp. 16–22.
